title: Rich Dad Poor Dad
author: Robert Kiyosaki
tagline: Escape the rat race. Make money work for you.

finalGrades.title: Poor Dad
finalGrades.blurb: Still trading time for money and hoping the paycheck stretches. Re-read the stations, the mindset shift is the whole game.

finalGrades.title: Middle Class
finalGrades.blurb: You know the rules now. You buy some assets, but doodads and liabilities still sneak in. Keep widening the gap between earning and owning.

finalGrades.title: Rich Dad
finalGrades.blurb: You think in assets and cashflow. Money works for you, fear doesn't run you, and you see opportunity where others see risk. Financially free.

lessons.heading: Two Dads, Two Mindsets
lessons.body:
Kiyosaki grew up with two father figures. His 'Poor Dad' — highly educated, secure job — believed the love of money is the root of all evil, and that you study hard to get a safe job.

His 'Rich Dad' — an entrepreneur who never finished school — believed the LACK of money is the root of all evil, and that you study hard to learn how money works so you can own businesses and assets.

Same son, two scripts. The whole book is about choosing which script runs your life.
lessons.quote: The poor and the middle class work for money. The rich have money work for them.

lessons.heading: What the Rat Race Really Is
lessons.body:
Most people get a job, earn a paycheck, pay bills, and get a raise — which they spend on a bigger house and nicer car, creating bigger bills. Earn more, spend more, owe more. Round and round.

Kiyosaki calls this the Rat Race: working harder and harder while staying in the same financial spot. Two emotions keep the wheel spinning — FEAR of not having money, and DESIRE for what money buys.

Financial education is how you step off the wheel.

questions.q: According to Rich Dad, the core difference between the rich and everyone else is:
options: The rich make money work for them, instead of working for money
The rich are luckier with investments
questions.explain: It is not about salary size. The rich build and buy assets that generate income, so their money does the working.

questions.q: Getting a higher-paying job is the surest way to escape the rat race.
questions.explain: A raise usually triggers more spending and bigger liabilities. Without financial education, more income just spins the wheel faster.

questions.q: Rich Dad would say the two emotions that trap most people in the rat race are:
questions.explain: Fear of being without money pushes you to work; desire for what money buys empties the paycheck. Master both and you control the wheel.

questions.q: Poor Dad said 'I can't afford it.' What did Rich Dad insist you ask instead?
questions.explain: 'I can't afford it' shuts the brain down. 'How can I afford it?' forces it to find a way — the muscle the rich exercise daily.

levels.title: The Rich Don't Work for Money

lessons.heading: Let Money Be the Teacher
lessons.body:
As a boy, Kiyosaki worked for Rich Dad for almost nothing. He got angry. Rich Dad let him stay angry on purpose — because that anger is what most people feel their whole lives but never examine.

The lesson: a job is something most people do out of fear. Working only for a paycheck keeps you emotionally controlled by the employer and by your bills. Rich Dad wanted Robert to see the trap from the inside while he was young.
lessons.quote: The poor and middle class work for money. Learn to have money work for you, and you are free.

lessons.heading: Fear and Greed Run the Show
lessons.body:
Rich Dad taught that most financial decisions are driven by two emotions: fear (of not paying the bills) and greed/desire (wanting more once the money arrives).

When a raise comes, fear and desire grab it instantly — new car, bigger house, more stuff. The paycheck rises but so do the obligations. The trick is to feel those emotions, then think instead of react. Your mind is your greatest asset; train it to spot opportunity rather than just chase security.

questions.q: Why did Rich Dad pay young Robert so little — and even pay him nothing for a while?
options: To make him feel the emotion (fear and anger) that controls most workers, so he would learn to think past it
Because the work had no value
questions.explain: It was a deliberate lesson. Feeling the trap of working for money — then choosing to think instead of react — is the whole point.

questions.q: Rich Dad believed your salary is your most important financial asset.
questions.explain: Your MIND is the greatest asset. Trained well, it builds wealth; untrained, it just chases a bigger paycheck.

questions.q: You just got a 20% raise. What would Rich Dad do?
options: Upgrade the car and apartment to match the new income
Direct the extra income into acquiring income-producing assets before lifestyle creeps up
Keep it all in checking for emergencies
questions.explain: The rich convert raises into assets, not bigger liabilities. Lifestyle inflation is exactly how fear and desire keep you running.

questions.q: Rich Dad said most people let two emotions make their money decisions. They are:
questions.explain: Fear drives you to take and keep a job; desire spends the reward. Awareness of both is the start of control.

lessons.heading: The One Rule: Know the Difference
lessons.body:
Rich Dad's Rule No. 1: you must know the difference between an asset and a liability, and buy assets.

His definitions are blunt and cashflow-based, not accountant-based:
• An ASSET puts money INTO your pocket.
• A LIABILITY takes money OUT of your pocket.

That's it. The rich buy assets. The poor have only expenses. The middle class buy liabilities they think are assets.
lessons.quote: Rich people acquire assets. The poor and middle class acquire liabilities that they think are assets.

lessons.heading: Three Cashflow Patterns
lessons.body:
Picture income flowing through a person:
• The POOR: income comes in and goes straight out to expenses. Nothing left.
• The MIDDLE CLASS: income buys liabilities — a mortgage, car loans, credit-card debt — that look like assets but drain cash every month.
• The RICH: income buys assets, and those assets throw off enough income to cover expenses. The asset column grows and feeds itself.

Wealth is measured by how long your assets could support you if you stopped working — not by how big your paycheck is.

questions.q: In Rich Dad's cashflow definition, an asset is something that:
options: The bank approves you to borrow for
questions.explain: Forget the accounting textbook. If it feeds your pocket each month, it's an asset; if it drains it, it's a liability.

questions.q: Rich Dad argued that the home you live in is usually a liability, not an asset.
questions.explain: Your residence takes money out of your pocket every month (mortgage, tax, upkeep). By his cashflow rule that makes it a liability — a famously controversial point.

questions.q: Sort these into Assets and Liabilities — Rich Dad style (does it put money IN, or take money OUT, of your pocket?).
questions.prompt: Drag each item into a bucket. On phone, tap an item then tap a bucket.

items.text: Royalties from a book you wrote
The mortgage on your own home

questions.explain: Assets generate income (rent, dividends, royalties). Liabilities pull cash out each month (loans, balances, your own mortgage).

questions.q: A friend brags that their brand-new financed luxury car is 'a great asset.' What would Rich Dad say?
options: Call it a liability: it takes money out of their pocket every month and loses value
Say it depends on the colour
questions.explain: A financed, depreciating car drains cash monthly. By the cashflow rule it's a liability, no matter how nice it looks.

questions.q: Rich Dad measured true wealth as:
options: How long you could survive on your assets' income if you stopped working
questions.explain: Wealth = days you can sustain your lifestyle from assets alone. A big paycheck with no assets is not wealth.

lessons.heading: Your Profession vs. Your Business
lessons.body:
Your profession is what pays your bills today — your job. Your business is what you build for wealth: your asset column.

Ray Kroc once asked a room of MBAs what business McDonald's was in. They said hamburgers. He said: 'No — my business is real estate.' The burgers paid the bills; the real estate built the fortune.

Keep your day job, but start minding your OWN business: relentlessly building assets on the side.
lessons.quote: The rich focus on their asset columns while everyone else focuses on their income statements.

lessons.heading: Beware the Doodads
lessons.body:
Rich Dad called the little luxuries that eat your money 'doodads' — gadgets, upgrades, impulse buys. They feel small, but they're where would-be assets go to die.

The discipline: every spare dollar buys a real asset first — income real estate, stocks, bonds, notes, intellectual property, anything that produces income or appreciates. Let the asset column grow until its income exceeds your expenses. Then, and only then, let the assets buy your luxuries.

questions.q: 'Mind your own business' in Rich Dad terms means:
options: Keep your job, but relentlessly build your own asset column on the side
questions.explain: Your profession pays bills; your 'business' — the asset column — builds wealth. Grow it alongside the job.

questions.q: Rich Dad keeps a tight asset column. Sort these into real Assets vs. money-draining Doodads/Liabilities.
questions.prompt: Drag each item into a bucket. On phone, tap an item then tap a bucket.

items.text: A second boat you rarely use

questions.explain: Assets produce income or appreciate (real estate, IP, stocks/bonds). Doodads are luxuries that just drain the asset column.

questions.q: Ray Kroc said the real business of McDonald's was selling hamburgers.
questions.explain: He said the real business was REAL ESTATE. The burgers paid the bills; the property under each store built the wealth.

questions.q: You have a spare $500 each month. What would Rich Dad do first?
options: Buy an income-producing asset and let it grow
Upgrade to a nicer phone and watch
questions.explain: Assets first, luxuries later — bought by the income the assets produce. That's how the column compounds.

lessons.heading: Earn, Spend, THEN Pay Tax
lessons.body:
Rich Dad pointed out a structural edge: employees earn, get taxed, and spend what's left. A corporation earns, spends on legitimate expenses, and is taxed on what remains.

Knowing how corporations and tax law work legally lets the financially educated keep more of what they make. It's not about cheating — it's about understanding the rules the system is actually written in.
lessons.quote: Each dollar in my asset column is a great employee, working hard to make more employees and buy the boss a new car.

lessons.heading: The Four Pillars of Financial IQ
lessons.body:
Rich Dad said financial intelligence rests on four kinds of knowledge:
1. ACCOUNTING — financial literacy; reading the numbers.
2. INVESTING — the science of money making money.
3. MARKETS — supply, demand, and what drives value.
4. THE LAW — tax, corporate, and legal rules that protect and accelerate wealth.

He also split debt in two: GOOD debt (someone else pays it, like a tenant covering a rental mortgage) and BAD debt (you pay it, out of your own pocket, for liabilities).

questions.q: Which is the correct order for a corporation, per Rich Dad's point about the tax edge?
options: Earn → spend (on expenses) → pay tax on what's left
questions.explain: Corporations are taxed on what remains after legitimate expenses; employees are taxed before they get to spend. Knowing the rules matters.

questions.q: Which is NOT one of Rich Dad's four pillars of financial intelligence?
questions.explain: The four are Accounting, Investing, Understanding Markets, and the Law. Networking is useful, but it isn't one of the four.

questions.q: Rich Dad calls a rental mortgage that your tenant's rent fully pays off:
options: Bad debt — debt is always bad
Good debt — someone else pays it and the asset is yours
questions.explain: Good debt is paid by someone else (the tenant) while you keep the appreciating asset. Bad debt comes out of your own pocket for liabilities.

questions.q: Rich Dad believed understanding tax law is about illegally hiding money.
questions.explain: It's about legally understanding the rules — corporations, deductions, structures — so you keep more of what you earn within the law.

lessons.heading: Courage Beats a Higher IQ
lessons.body:
Rich Dad said the single most powerful asset is the mind — and what holds most minds back is not a lack of intelligence but a lack of courage. Self-doubt and fear of losing keep people on the sidelines.

Opportunities are everywhere; the financially intelligent are trained to SEE them and bold enough to ACT. Often it's not the smartest person who wins, but the one willing to take calculated risk while others freeze.
lessons.quote: Often in the real world, it's not the smart who get ahead, but the bold.

lessons.heading: Money Is Invented
lessons.body:
Great financial opportunities are not found — they're created by trained minds. Where an average investor sees a run-down house, a trained one sees a deal to structure. Where others see a falling market, the financially intelligent see assets on sale.

Kiyosaki's two types of investors: those who buy packaged investments, and those who CREATE investments by assembling deals. Building financial intelligence — and the courage to use it — is how you join the second group and effectively 'invent' money.

questions.q: Rich Dad said what most often holds people back from wealth is:
options: Lack of courage — fear and self-doubt — not lack of intelligence
questions.explain: Intelligence is common; courage to act on opportunity is rarer. Fear of losing keeps capable people from ever starting.

questions.q: A market crashes and prices fall sharply. What would Rich Dad most likely see?
options: Assets on sale — an opportunity for the prepared and financially intelligent
A reason to never invest again
questions.explain: Trained investors see downturns as discounts. 'Inventing money' means acting with intelligence where others react with fear.

questions.q: Rich Dad believed great opportunities are simply found by luck, not created by skill.
questions.explain: The rich INVENT money — they create and structure deals using financial intelligence. It's a trained skill, not luck.

questions.q: Kiyosaki describes two types of investors. The wealthier mindset is the one who:
options: Creates investments by assembling and structuring deals
Waits for a financial advisor to decide everything
questions.explain: Deal-creators assemble opportunities (the components: the deal, the money, the smart people). That's how the rich 'invent' money.

levels.title: Work to Learn, Not to Earn

lessons.heading: Chase Skills, Not Salaries
lessons.body:
Young Kiyosaki was advised by Rich Dad to take jobs for what he would LEARN, not what he would earn. He even recommended a sales job at Xerox — not for the pay, but to conquer his fear of rejection and master selling.

The most important skills for building wealth aren't narrow specialties. They're broad: sales, marketing, communication, and understanding how systems and people work together.
lessons.quote: Job security meant everything to my educated dad. Learning meant everything to my rich dad.

lessons.heading: Specialists vs. Generalists
lessons.body:
School trains specialists — go deep in one narrow field and get paid for that expertise. Rich Dad valued the opposite for wealth-building: 'know a little about a lot.'

The two skills he prized most were SELLING and MARKETING — the ability to communicate value and move people to act. Many brilliant, highly-educated people stay poor because they can't sell. Work to learn the skills that let you build and run businesses, even if it means a lower paycheck for a while.

questions.q: Rich Dad advised young Robert to choose jobs based primarily on:
options: What skills he would learn from them
How close they were to home
questions.explain: Work to LEARN, not to earn. Early jobs are for acquiring skills that build wealth later, not maximizing the paycheck today.

questions.q: You're offered two jobs: one pays more but teaches nothing new; the other pays less but builds your sales and leadership skills. Rich Dad would pick:
options: The higher-paying job — always take the money
The lower-paying job that builds transferable, wealth-building skills
Neither — wait for a perfect offer
questions.explain: Skills compound for a lifetime; a slightly bigger early paycheck doesn't. Choose the role that teaches you to build.

questions.q: Rich Dad believed the most valuable wealth-building skills are deep, narrow specializations.
questions.explain: He prized GENERALIST skills — selling, marketing, communication, leadership — 'know a little about a lot.' Specialists often stay employees.

questions.q: Which pair of skills did Rich Dad consider most essential for building wealth?
questions.explain: The ability to sell and market — to communicate value and move people to act — underpins almost every successful business.

lessons.heading: The Boss Level
lessons.body:
This is the final exam — a mix of everything from the Path to Financial Freedom. No new reading.

Prove you can think like Rich Dad: assets vs. liabilities, fear and desire, building your own business, the four pillars, inventing money, and working to learn.

Score 85%+ to earn the rank of Rich Dad. Roll the dice.
lessons.quote: It's not how much money you make. It's how much money you keep, and how many generations you keep it for.

questions.q: The single defining habit of the rich, per the book, is:
options: Saving every penny in a bank
questions.explain: Wealth is built by buying assets that put money in your pocket — the asset column is the engine.

questions.q: By Rich Dad's cashflow definition, the home you live in is typically a liability.
questions.explain: It takes money out of your pocket monthly, so by the pocket test it's a liability — the book's most debated claim.

questions.q: Final sort: Assets vs. Liabilities, Rich Dad style.
questions.prompt: Drag each item into a bucket. On phone, tap an item then tap a bucket.
questions.explain: Money IN = asset (rental, royalties). Money OUT = liability (credit-card debt, financed car).

questions.q: A recession hits and asset prices tumble. The Rich Dad mindset reacts by:
options: Looking for quality assets now on sale
questions.explain: Downturns are discounts for the prepared. Inventing money means acting with intelligence while others freeze in fear.

questions.q: Rich Dad's four pillars of financial intelligence are accounting, investing, understanding markets, and:
questions.explain: Accounting, Investing, Markets, and the Law — together they let you build and protect wealth.

questions.q: Rich Dad advised taking early jobs to LEARN skills, not just to maximize earnings.
questions.explain: Work to learn, not to earn. Skills like selling and marketing compound into wealth-building ability.

questions.q: You receive a surprise bonus. The most Rich Dad move is to:
options: Buy an income-producing asset before lifestyle creep takes it
Spend it on a vacation you'll finance
questions.explain: Convert windfalls into assets first. Let the assets — not the paycheck — eventually buy the luxuries.
